title: On the run for 20 years, most-wanted fugitive caught hiding as a biotech executive
gist: A fugitive convicted in absentia of sexual assault built a fabricated oncology-executive persona over 20 years that got him hired as interim chief medical officer at two public biotech companies, whose headline credential claims neither company could verify.

themes.theme: Executive vetting failures at public biotechs

insights.claim: Immix Biopharma hired 'Richard Graydon' as interim chief medical officer in March, describing him in its announcement as a board-certified hematologist-oncologist with over 20 years of experience who had led new-drug and biologics-license applications at Merck & Co. and Johnson & Johnson for seven approved drugs including Darzalex, Carvykti, Keytruda, and Imbruvica.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Shows how a fabricated but specific-sounding pharma resume, naming real bestselling drugs and real companies, was enough to secure a chief medical officer title at a public company.

insights.claim: Stat News reported it could not verify any of the credentials Immix publicized for Richard Graydon, including a claimed MD and PhD from Stanford University and training at Harvard's Massachusetts General Hospital, and Immix did not respond to requests for comment.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Highlights a real due-diligence gap: prestigious-sounding credentials attached to a hire were never independently confirmed before or after being publicized.
insights.quote: Stat could not verify any of that information, and Immix did not respond to requests for comment.

insights.claim: Atossa Therapeutics, a Seattle-based biotech, hired Richard Graydon as interim chief medical officer in October 2022, with CEO Steven Quay praising his 'deep experience in CAR-T cell therapy' in a company statement.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Shows the same fabricated persona was used to obtain a second, similarly senior clinical role at a different public biotech years before the Immix hire.
insights.quote: We are privileged to welcome Richard to Atossa, where his deep experience in CAR-T cell therapy fits well with our current strategic direction as we explore our opportunities in cell therapy.

insights.claim: Richard Graydon self-published a 2017 book, 'The Genetic Risks of Cancer: The Effects of DNA, Genomics and Inheritance on Aging and Survival,' which described him as an oncologist and biotech chief medical officer; the book had no reviews on Amazon.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Illustrates a deliberate, years-long tactic of manufacturing a paper trail of professional legitimacy that later hiring companies apparently took at face value.

insights.claim: Richard Graydon was an alias for Ronald Fischer, a 70-year-old former anesthesiologist whose medical licenses in Rhode Island and Massachusetts were revoked after a 1994 sexual assault conviction, and who fled the US during his 2005 trial for first-degree sexual assault.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: The fugitive rebuilt an entire medical-executive career under a new identity despite having already lost his actual medical license before fleeing.

insights.claim: Before his 2005 trial concluded, Fischer emailed his lawyer saying he had decided not to risk conviction and had been 'carefully planning a good, safe, secure and comfortable life' abroad, then fled and was convicted in absentia.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Confirms the flight was premeditated well before the verdict, not a panicked reaction to conviction.
insights.quote: I have therefore decided not to take the risk and leave the US and enjoy life in another country where I have long been carefully planning a good, safe, secure and comfortable life.

insights.claim: Police had identified more than a dozen aliases used by Fischer during his 20 years as a fugitive, and his police profile described him as a 'master yachtsman, a world traveler, and internationally connected,' having previously been featured on America's Most Wanted.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Suggests a sustained, resourced pattern of identity-switching rather than a single one-off alias.

insights.claim: Fischer, using the Graydon alias, was arrested July 16 aboard a 56-foot sailing vessel called The Silver Lining on New York's East River, intercepted by US Marshals and the US Coast Guard 'without incident.'
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Ends a 20-year manhunt and confirms the fugitive maintained a maritime lifestyle consistent with his pre-flight profile as a yachtsman.

insights.claim: In its SEC filing disclosing Graydon's termination, Immix Biopharma stated he was let go for 'reasons unrelated to his activities at the Company' and said that, given his short tenure, there was no material effect on the business.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Shows how a company can disclose a fugitive-identity termination in minimal, reputation-shielding language rather than acknowledging the fraud directly.
insights.quote: Given his short tenure, management believes there is no material effect on the business.
